retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Bronchospasm -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Wheezing is a common presenting symptom in the preschool age group. However, the majority of these children do not develop a chronic, persistent form of asthma. The odds of developing early childhood asthma are two to three times higher in infants with one or more episodes of bronchiolitis. [18] Infants requiring hospitalization for bronchiolitis have higher early childhood asthma morbidity. [18] Epidemiologic studies have aided in the development of an Asthma Predictive Index to improve the accuracy of diagnosing children younger than three years old with asthma. The Asthma Predictive Index is considered positive if a child has had at least three episodes of wheezing per year, plus meets either one major criterion (parental asthma, atopic dermatitis, or sensitization to air allergens) or two minor criteria (food allergies, wheezing unrelated to “colds”, or eosinophilia greater than or equal to 4%). A positive Asthma Predictive Index means the child is at a significantly higher risk of having asthma later in life. As high as 76% of toddlers with a positive Asthma Predictive Index will have active asthma symptoms when they are school age. [19] [20] In regards to the prognosis in anaphylaxis, early identification and treatment with epinephrine are critical to stopping the progression of anaphylaxis to respiratory or cardiac arrest and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Not all cases of wheezing indicate asthma. Clinicians must remember that wheezing has a broad differential. Clinicians should be cautious with patients exhibiting significant respiratory distress despite apparently normal blood gas levels, as well as those who appear lethargic or altered, as they may be approaching respiratory failure and subsequent cardiac arrest. Patients who necessitate continuous nebulized treatments or interventions beyond standard medications, such as albuterol, ipratropium, and steroids, may be experiencing status asthmatics. This requires hospital admission for comprehensive evaluation and treatment. Importantly, clinicians should investigate common asthma triggers, such as upper respiratory tract infections, allergens, and exercise, as well as less common triggers, such as gastroesophageal reflux, medications, and psychological distress

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation may manage symptoms at home or require urgent medical care, depending on severity and risk factors for fatal asthma. Urgent medical attention is needed for those with marked shortness of breath, inability to speak more than short phrases, use of accessory muscles, mental status changes, or at high risk of fatal exacerbation, which requires urgent medical attention. Risk factors that predict a fatal asthma exacerbation include: Previous life-threatening exacerbation Exacerbation despite OCS use Multiple emergency room visits (3 or more) or hospitalizations (more than 1) in the last year Use of more than 1 SABA MDI per month Food allergies Chronic heart or lung disease Medication nonadherence Psychosocial stresssors

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Severe asthma exacerbations can lead to life-threatening complications, particularly when treatment is delayed or inadequate. Common complications include respiratory failure, dynamic hyperinflation, and barotrauma, such as pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum. Prolonged hypoxia may result in altered mental status, cardiac arrhythmias, or, rarely, cardiopulmonary arrest. Medication-related adverse effects, including tachyarrhythmias or hypotension from β2-agonists or epinephrine, may also occur. Timely recognition and intervention by EMS are critical in preventing clinical deterioration and improving outcomes in the prehospital phase.

retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis of asthma Stepwise approach for management of asthma Emphasis on the use of preferred treatment at each step SMART (single maintenance and reliever therapy) before proceeding to a higher-step level of treatment. [26] Achieve adequate symptom control Management of asthma exacerbation Minimize adverse drug reactions Shared decision-making accounting for risks vs. benefits and personal preferences Patient education regarding inhaler techniques Improving patient adherence Assessment of environmental triggers Evaluation and management of comorbidities Overall reduction in mortality Assessment of quality of life by using a validated questionnaire (Pediatric Asthma Quality of Life Questionnaire) Consultation with a pulmonologist/immunologist if the patient requires care at step 3 or higher. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Evaluation -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Additional laboratory studies, such as arterial blood gas analysis, may be warranted in patients with worsening symptoms despite treatment or in critically ill patients. However, these tests should not delay the initiation of bronchodilator therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Evaluation -- Acute Exacerbation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians may also assess severity using peak flow measurement, although this method is less common in children. Children aged 6 or younger may struggle to perform these measurements accurately, and very ill children may be unable to provide 3 readings. Asthma exacerbations are diagnosed clinically and do not require routine laboratory or imaging studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation with spirometry, complemented by postbronchodilator response, is pivotal for establishing a definitive diagnosis. Spirometry aids in identifying various respiratory issues in children, including airway obstruction, restrictive lung disease, chest wall and respiratory muscle defects, diffusion defects, and respiratory muscle weakness. Although airborne agents typically dominate discussions of asthma triggers, a diverse range of stimuli, such as respiratory infections, allergen exposure, environmental irritants, physical activity, hormonal fluctuations, medications, and psychosocial factors, can precipitate or worsen symptoms. Treatment strategies revolve around educating patients and caregivers, regularly monitoring symptoms, and ensuring access to both fast-acting bronchodilators and appropriate controller medications tailored to disease severity. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The cornerstone of diagnostic evaluation is spirometry, which aids in identifying chest wall, respiratory muscle defects, and respiratory muscle weakness. This technique also assesses airflow limitation and excludes alternative diagnoses. Asthma triggers extend beyond airborne agents, including respiratory infections, allergen exposure, environmental irritants, physical activity, hormonal fluctuations, medications, and psychosocial factors. Treatment strategies focus on patient and caregiver education, regular symptom monitoring, and the implementation of stepwise tailored medication regimens based on symptom severity. This activity covers the etiology, pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of pediatric asthma. This activity provides healthcare professionals with the information and resources to improve long-term outcomes and reduce the risk of future asthma attacks, impaired lung development, decreased lung function, and structural changes in children's lungs affected by asthma.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric asthma is characterized by variable expiratory airway limitation and persistent respiratory symptoms, including wheezing, coughing, shortness of breath, and chest tightness. Airway hyperresponsiveness and inflammation are additional common features, with severity ranging from intermittent symptoms to potentially life-threatening airway compromise. Asthma often occurs in childhood and is influenced by both genetics and environmental factors. Most children who wheeze before age 6 will likely have their symptoms resolved. However, a subset of children, typically those with atopy, a family history of asthma, and persistent symptoms at a young age, may develop persistent asthma. Assessment evaluates symptom intensity, exacerbation risk, and the likelihood of persistent asthma, guiding treatment decisions accordingly.

retrieved_docs.content: SABA overuse has been associated with increased risks of exacerbation. Long-term use of β-2 agonists has been associated with ischemia, heart failure, arrhythmias, and risk of sudden death. [65] A high dose of ICS is associated with systemic ADR due to increased absorption. It may increase the risk of adrenal suppression, cataracts, glaucoma, psychiatric effects, and risk of infection. [66]

retrieved_docs.content: Airway remodeling Decreased physical activity and deconditioning Osteoporosis, cataracts, and glaucoma due to glucocorticoids Reduced growth in children with long-term use of nasal or inhaled corticosteroids Poor work and school productivity

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma -- Evaluation -- Identification of Patients at Risk of Fatal or Near-Fatal Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Most asthma-related deaths are preventable if risk factors are identified and addressed early. Major risk factors that place patients at high risk for future fatal asthma exacerbations include: A recent history of poorly controlled asthma A prior history of near-fatal asthma A history of endotracheal intubation for asthma A history of intensive care unit admission for asthma

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients on chronic systemic steroids for asthma have a suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis. A sudden withdrawal of steroids can result in adrenal insufficiency. Steroid withdrawal syndrome is marked by malaise, postural hypotension, generalized weakness, arthralgia, headaches, mood swings, and emotional lability and can be life-threatening if not recognized promptly. A gradual steroid taper is recommended. [67]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is a major cause of morbidity and mortality in the United States. Numerous studies have shown that early institution of inhaled corticosteroids can reduce exacerbations and decrease the frequency of associated hospital admissions. This activity outlines the diagnosis, management and treatment of environmental and allergic asthma and describes how to prevent exacerbations of it. It highlights the importance of the interprofessional team in helping educate patients about compliance with treatment and prevention of exacerbations by identifying the triggers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2926
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure and elevated systolic blood pressure are both classified as vascular abnormalities, reflecting increased pressure within the circulatory system. The broader vascular category also encompasses severe pressure disorders such as أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate a spectrum of vascular pressure elevations that are important for clinicians to recognize, as they can signal acute emergencies or chronic pulmonary vascular disease and guide appropriate monitoring and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure are all classified as Vascular conditions, indicating that each represents a form of increased arterial pressure within the vascular system. The Vascular category also encompasses أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) and ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension). Together, these entities illustrate the spectrum of vascular hypertension, ranging from chronic elevations in diastolic, systolic, and mean arterial pressures to acute severe hypertension and pulmonary‑specific hypertension, and recognizing them as children of the Vascular classification aids clinicians in organizing assessment and treatment strategies for hypertension‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2734
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists, whose mechanism of action involves blocking β‑adrenergic receptors, have a contraindicated mechanism of action with methacholin, a muscarinic receptor agonist. Because the pharmacologic effects of β‑blockade oppose the cholinergic stimulation produced by methacholin—potentially leading to adverse outcomes such as enhanced bronchoconstriction or undesirable cardiovascular effects—these agents should not be used together in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Apnea (الاِختِنَاق) is the overarching respiratory disturbance that encompasses the broader category of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ). Within this category, the clinical condition described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) represents a more severe manifestation. Thus, adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are subtypes of the respiratory disorder, which itself is nested under the primary concept of apnea, illustrating a hierarchical relationship that links a fundamental breathing abnormality to increasingly critical respiratory pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808
retrieved_docs.content: Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is defined by the presence of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure, each of which is a direct manifestation of this condition. Within the vascular domain, these elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular children, reflecting the systemic impact of primary hypertension on the circulatory system. Primary hypertension is also mapped to several related vascular entities: hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). In addition, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and is clinically linked to abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). Together, these relationships illustrate how primary hypertension not only elevates key blood pressure parameters but also predisposes patients to acute vascular emergencies, distinct hypertension subtypes, pulmonary vascular disease, and radiographic abnormalities, underscoring the importance of comprehensive vascular assessment and monitoring in patient care.
